title: scanRegistry conflates all readdir errors with no-paths-dir
description: The try/catch around fs.readdirSync uses an anonymous catch (no err binding) and unconditionally returns the 'no-paths-dir' warning. EACCES (permission denied), ENOTDIR (paths/ is a regular file), EMFILE/ENFILE (descriptor exhaustion), and ELOOP (symlink loop) all collapse to the same misleading 'Paths directory not found' message. The original error message is dropped entirely.
impact: When a user's paths/ directory exists but is unreadable (chmod 000, owned by another user, replaced with a file), the start tool tells them the directory is missing. Diagnosis path is wrong; the user will look for a missing directory rather than fix the permissions or the misnamed file. Test T-025 only exercises the genuine ENOENT case, so this misclassification ships unobserved.
recommendation: Bind the error (catch (err)) and branch on err.code: ENOENT -> 'no-paths-dir'; ENOTDIR -> a new 'paths-not-a-directory' kind; EACCES -> 'paths-dir-unreadable'; otherwise a generic 'paths-dir-error' kind that includes err.message in the warning. Preserve the original message so the user can act on it.

title: probeOutputStyle reports any read failure as settings-file-missing
description: The catch around fs.readFileSync(settingsPath, 'utf8') has no err binding and unconditionally returns the 'settings-file-missing' warning. EACCES (settings.json owned by another user / chmod 000), EISDIR (settings.json is a directory), ENOTDIR (~/.claude is a file), and any other I/O failure all surface as 'Settings file not found'.
impact: The student cannot diagnose why the learning-output-style plugin is not detected. They see 'file not found' and create the file, but the real problem is permissions or a typo elsewhere in the path. Because outputStyleOk is advisory in cycle 1, the consequence is mild now, but the same probe is the load-bearing input to the AC-1.3 invariant in later cycles.
recommendation: Bind the error (catch (err)) and either include err.message in the warning, or branch on err.code to emit distinct warning kinds for ENOENT vs EACCES vs EISDIR. At minimum, append `: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}` to the message field, mirroring the pattern already used a few lines below for JSON.parse failures.

title: scanRegistry mislabels read errors on path.json as malformed JSON
description: When fs.readFileSync(pathJsonFile, ...) throws (EACCES on the file, EISDIR if path.json is a directory, etc.), the warning emitted has kind 'malformed-path-json'. The same kind is used for genuine JSON.parse failures a few lines later. A reader cannot distinguish 'I cannot read your file' from 'your file is not valid JSON'.
impact: The user is told their JSON is malformed and goes to lint/reformat it, when the real issue is filesystem. Diagnostic friction; not a correctness bug because the path is correctly skipped either way.
recommendation: Use a distinct warning kind for read failures, e.g. 'unreadable-path-json', and reserve 'malformed-path-json' for JSON.parse failures only. Include err.code if available.

title: existsSync precheck on path.json invites TOCTOU and EACCES misclassification
description: fs.existsSync(pathJsonFile) is used to decide between emitting 'missing-path-json' and proceeding to readFileSync. existsSync returns false on EACCES (cannot stat the parent), so a permission-denied case reports as 'missing-path-json' even when the file is present. It also creates a TOCTOU window: the file may be deleted between existsSync and readFileSync, causing a different warning kind ('malformed-path-json' per the read-error path). The two warnings disagree on what is wrong.
impact: Inconsistent diagnostics for the same underlying state, depending on race timing and permission setup. The cycle-1 surface is small enough that this is unlikely to bite, but it is a smell that compounds when registries grow.
recommendation: Drop the existsSync precheck. Call fs.readFileSync inside the try/catch and branch on err.code in the catch: ENOENT -> 'missing-path-json'; EACCES -> 'unreadable-path-json'; default -> generic with err.message. Single source of truth and no race window.

title: probeOutputStyle silently returns ok=false when settings shape is wrong
description: If settings.json parses but the top level is a non-object (array, string, number) or enabledPlugins is the wrong type (string, array, null), the probe returns { ok: false } with NO warning. T-017 requires this for the 'enabledPlugins absent' case, but the same code path also masks structurally-broken settings files. A user who accidentally writes `enabledPlugins: "true"` (string) gets no diagnostic — start just reports outputStyleOk=false.
impact: Silent-disable for misconfigured settings looks identical to silent-disable for an absent key. The user has no breadcrumb. Low because the field is advisory in cycle 1; the asymmetry becomes louder in later cycles.
recommendation: Distinguish the absent-enabledPlugins case (no warning, intentional silent-disable per contract) from the wrong-shape case ('settings-file-malformed' or a new 'settings-shape-invalid' warning). Document which shapes are intentional silent-disable and which are diagnostic.

title: MCP server top-level await crashes process on transport failure
description: `await server.connect(transport)` is the last line of the module. If the stdio transport fails (closed stdin, parent died, EPIPE), the unhandled rejection terminates the Node process with a stack trace rather than a clean error message. There is also no try/catch around runStart inside the tool handler — a thrown error would bubble back to the MCP layer as a generic protocol error rather than as a structured tool result.
impact: Operationally low because Claude Code generally manages the server lifecycle, but a torn-down parent will produce a noisy stack trace in logs and the user sees no useful diagnostic. The runStart wrap is a safety net for future cycles when registry/probe code grows.
recommendation: Wrap the connect call in try/catch and log a single-line structured message before re-throwing or exit(1). Wrap the body of the start tool handler in try/catch so unexpected errors return a structured failure result instead of bubbling.
evidence:
const transport = new StdioServerTransport();
await server.connect(transport);

title: Schema validators fail-fast; user fixes one error at a time
description: validatePath returns on the first error encountered. A path.json missing both `slug` and `title` reports only 'Missing required field: slug'; the user fixes that, runs again, then hears about title; etc. validatePhases (schemas/phases.ts) has the same pattern. The contract does not require aggregated errors for cycle 1, so this is conformant — but the contract test T-035 only checks the single missing-slug case, which is exactly the behaviour that ships.
impact: Author UX during course development: each malformed path.json takes N round-trips to diagnose where a single round-trip with all errors would suffice. Not a runtime-correctness issue.
recommendation: Optional cycle-2+ enhancement: collect all errors into an array before returning. Switch the validation result type to { ok: false; errors: string[] }. Not required for cycle 1.

title: Symlinked path directories are silently dropped
description: dirEntries filters with `e.isDirectory()`, which returns false for a symlink that points to a directory (Dirent.isDirectory() reports the link's own type, not the target). A developer who symlinks paths/04-fake-path -> /tmp/work/fake-path during local iteration gets no entry and no warning — the path silently disappears.
impact: Niche but plausible during course authoring. The omission is silent (no warning), violating the spirit of the contract's 'malformed paths are skipped with a structured warning'.
recommendation: Either follow symlinks (use fs.statSync(slugDir).isDirectory() or readdirSync with { withFileTypes: true } and treat e.isSymbolicLink() as a candidate, then stat-resolve), or emit a low-severity warning when a symlink is encountered so authors know it was skipped.
